sentences.text: Well, this is all recently, right?
Recently, United States, not back to Belgium.
We don't have a relationship at this point.
I haven't seen my brother since 1979, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was a very sad occasion again in my life.
The funeral of my son, Fred.
After-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after I got divorced I had custody of my son and he was doing very bad in school.
He had very bad grades.
So I decided that I was going to put him in a private school.
And I enrolled him in a private school here on Lochmond-- Page Military Academy.
And within a year, he turned out to be a straight-A student, with the help of Page Military Academy.
And he went there through his junior high school years.
And after that, when he graduated, we enrolled him in a school in Lake Elsinore to finish his high school-- another military academy, Lake Elsinore Military Academy.
After one year, they closed their doors, and he was enrolled with a partial-- partial scholarship with the Army, Navy Academy in Carlsbad, which he attended for two years and graduated with honors.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And decided to make a career in the Air Force.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He enlisted in the Air Force, and finishes boot camp-- Texas, I believe.
He was transferred to Cheyenne, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] being a paramedic.
After some time, again, he was promoted to Corporal, and they moved him to Hawaii.
And in Hawaii he was-- two or three years, he was in Hawaii.
And he was, again, promoted to Sergeant.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He met a girl in Hawaii and he wanted to marry her.
And we tried to talk him out of it because she had-- he only had known her for about a month or two months, he didn't know her too well.
And I told him, be careful.
But he married her anyhow because the reason he got married, he was being transferred back to the mainland here and they were going to station him in Idaho, Mountain Home Air Force Base.
And he didn't want to leave her behind.
So he married her, in May of 1978.
And in July of 1978, he was transferred to Mountain Home Air Force Base.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was in the process of being checked in and he rented a place on the base.
And on his way to Idaho with his wife to buy furniture, on a Saturday morning, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he was hit head-on by another oncoming car, and a drunk driver.
[PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]
Killed him instantly, and his wife.
[CRYING] We had his body flown over to Los Angeles for a funeral.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And he had a military funeral with military honors.
And my brother came to attend the funeral.
I don't know why.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because he said he wanted to say, good bye, or honor Fred.
My thought always was he should've done it while he was alive, which he didn't.
And the only thing my brother could say to me after the funeral was, Jack, hang in there.
Then he left, and I haven't seen him since.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My son's death [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] affected the whole family.
My wife, Juliet, she treated him like her own son.
[CRYING] Olivia, it was her big brother.
The next time was too [INAUDIBLE], and she didn't realize what had happened.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] It still hurts.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My daughter, Karen, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she can't make herself go to his grave site.
She can't talk about him.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] There's been tragedy, again.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
The man who was accused of killing my son and my daughter-in-law lost consciousness.
And on the way to the hospital, he came out of his consciousness.
And he asked a policeman what happened to the other people?
Policeman said I think that they didn't survive.
Yeah, he said, because I'm the lucky one.
And he said something to the effect of, it was very stupid of me.
And he told the story without even being asked.
He told a highway patrolmen what exactly had happened.
His wife had an affair with one of the soldiers on the base.
And she had gone to town, to Boise, and he was at home by himself and was getting loaded on beer.
And he decided to go to Boise.
And on his way-- on the freeway to Boise, he saw his wife coming in the opposite direction, with her boyfriend sitting next to her.
So he lost his cool and made a U-turn on the freeway.
And went after her, going at 75, 85 miles an hour, trying to push her off the road.
Lost control of his car and crossed five lines of the highway and hit my son head on.
That's what he told the policeman.
He was accused of double voluntary manslaughter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the first trial, it ended in a hung jury.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the prosecutor brought up the fact, what he had said in the ambulance while he recovered, that was not admissible because they didn't read his rights.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
They had a second trial.
And I asked the prosecutor, would it be of any help if I'm there?
She said, yes, it may influence the jury a little bit, maybe.
I don't know.
I said, OK, I'll be there.
So I flew to Idaho, and I attended the trial.
I was there three days.
And the whole thing was just like a comedy.
The whole time was a big comedy.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
One of the main people who was-- the woman's boyfriend, he was called on the stand.
And he lost his memory.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They had-- the prosecutor had an expert flown in from Detroit, on-- it was a Ford camper, because he claimed there was something wrong with his steering column, or brake, or I don't know what.
The Ford man said, such a thing was impossible with this particular car.
His wife refused to testify against him.
She was in the process of getting a divorce.
Two weeks before the trial, the second trial, he was arrested for drunk driving.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Something went wrong with the blood alcohol test machine so they couldn't establish his blood alcohol.
Anyhow, the trial ended again in a hung jury.
The prosecuting attorney asked for a third trial.
And I asked for a third trial, because I wanted to have a clear judgment of guilty or not guilty.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The request was denied by the presiding judge.
He had all charges dismissed.
And in his dismissal, he wrote in the papers that it would be-- it would be unfair to the defendant to have a trial after trial by the state.
The state has unlimited funds, and it would be a financial burden on the defendant.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So that was the end of the trial.
He never got another trial.
He never got thrown out of the Air Force, because it happened off the base and the Air Force said it's a civil case, we can't do anything about it.
I wrote letters to the Air Force.
There's nothing they can do about it.
So he got no punishments whatsoever.
The only punishment he got was his wife divorced him.
[PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Yeah, that was in 1979, and we have been trying to rebuild our lives ever since.
We have some happy times now.
Karen getting married to a good husband, a beautiful grandchild.
Olivia getting married to a good husband, beautiful grandson.
For that I have to thank my wife, Juliet, because she introduced me, when I met her, to the Jewish life-- not religion, the Jewish life.

She-- her family was not a very religious family, either, but her sister married a very religious man, and through him, because of him, the whole family went towards going from the traditional more toward the religious part.
So now are there-- are there things that you do as a family that are more traditional and Jewish?
Well, we go to shul more often, and Juliet has a kosher kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We celebrate the holidays, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, Hanukkah.
Both of the children went to private Jewish schools.
Olivia and Vanessa both went first to the temple-- they started at temple-- is it Beth Am or Beth-El here on Fountain?
I don't know what the name of it is.
From there, they went to Temple Emanuel.
And when they graduated there, then they went to Herzl School.
And from Herzl School, they went both to public high school.
And after public high school, Olivia went to University of Northridge, and she got a degree in child care family therapy.
And Vanessa got a bachelor's degree from the university-- the state of-- San Diego State University, in public relations and journalism.
Yes, I did.
Oh, absolutely.
Absolutely.
I have dreams about it, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think about it a lot.
For some reason, I always-- I should forget, but I always try to go back with my memory to the past.
And I'm trying to recall things which have happened.
Sometimes Juliet asked me, why are you doing this?
Why are you punishing yourself?
I don't know.

But sometimes, you know, I go to bed at night and I'm thinking, and I'm forcing myself, trying to remember what happened on a particular day or a particular date or a particular year, so I shouldn't forget.
It's been haunting me all my life.
It still is.
How does this make you feel about people, having been treated so cruelly by the people who were supposed to be defending you?
How do I feel toward them?
I like people.
I like their company.
That's why I enjoyed my business, because I had a constant flow of different people coming in all day long.
I loved that.
I loved to be among people.
I get very upset when it comes to anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had one customer who came in once.
I had to develop a roll of film for him.
And he had dressed himself in a Nazi uniform with a helmet, with a Swastika painted on it.
And when he came in, I told him I don't want to work for you no more.
I said, go someplace else.
I said, this is no joke.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Like my daughter told me that, last week she was walking with her baby and her husband on the street, and a well-dressed man walked by in the opposite direction.
And my son, he wears a kippah.
He's a very-- very orthodox religious, my son-in-law, and my daughter.
And when he passed them, he said-- he said to them, swine.
My son-in-law, the only thing he said was, oh, really?
If it had been me, I don't know what I would have done.
I told my wife the same thing.
I probably would have kicked him somewhere.
I don't know, but I don't have to take that anymore.
Never again.
I want to ask you if there was anything else that you'd like to add, perhaps something that we forgot or any incidences that you'd like to include in this tape?
There are so many instances of what happened in Westmalle.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I recall one instance-- we had a terrible winter, and I had no coat.
And he was a very tall man.
So they gave me his coat, which was much too big for me.
It was hanging all the way to my shoes.
Winter coat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And at one evening, I wanted to go and see a play, because I belonged to a Catholic organization, youth group, and they had put on a play on the stage.
And I was going to see-- and see it.
And I was warming myself around the stove-- it was cold outside-- and I burned my coat in the back.
It had a huge, big, brown spot on it, and I didn't know about it.
So I went home.
And of course, Sunday morning when we had to go to church, he was the big Mr.
De Cock with his wife and his whole clan in front of him, walking to the church.
He was proud of that, to show that everybody was going to church Sunday morning.
And I was wearing that coat.
And he called me and he said, what happened to the coat?
I said, I don't know.
Of course he started accusing me of lying again, and he hit me in my face and he said, tell me the truth.
I said, I don't know what happened.
Anyhow, he made me take off the coat and bring it home, and I had to go to-- to church and walk on the street without a coat during the rest of the winter.
He said, I'll teach you to ruin clothing.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Do you wish me to go to the-- They had an abbey there, Trappists, and he wanted me to go down there every week and beg for a loaf of bread and cheese.

And I had to tell them-- he had prepared a little speech for me that I am the Jewish boy who was staying with the De Cock family, and that he'd converted me to Catholicism and he needed some help to feed me.
So every week, I had to walk-- and it was about an hour and a half walk one way.
I came home with a loaf of bread and a chunk of cheese, every week for years.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And I wrote to my brother after the war that he tried to get a medal for saving two Jews, and my brother stopped them from giving the medal.
But I don't know the details on that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But there are so many instances, you know.
It's-- it's-- I could go on and on and on.
It's-- seven years of abuse.
I would tell them, be proud to be a Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And don't let happen [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what happened to your forefathers, who were dragged away like cattle, who didn't put up a fight.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Don't let it happen again.
The person on the left is my grandfather-- grandfather on my father's side.
His name is Joseph Voorzanger.
Sitting next to him is my brother, Joe, and the next person is my grandmother, Heintje Voorzanger.
And next to her is my biological mother, Elizabeth, who is at this time pregnant with me.
That picture was taken in the Hague, approximately 1930.
This is my biological mother, Elizabeth.
I have no idea when this picture was taken or where.
I got this picture from an old photograph, and I made a copy.
I got all these pictures from my brother, who gave them to me, including old negatives, which he saved during the war years.
And after the war, when I was working at a photo lab, I printed them and enlarged them and made copies.
The man on the left, of course, is my father, Solomon Voorzanger, putting his hands on my shoulder.
I'm here.
I think about six or seven years old.
Next to me is my mother, Rosetta, and my brother Joe, who is six years older than I am.
This picture was taken in Belgium, in Antwerp, 1936, '37, approximately.
This picture, the woman on the left is my grandmother, the mother of my stepmother.
And next to her is my brother, Joe, and then of course, my stepmother, Rosetta, and myself.
This picture also was taken in the same city of Antwerp, 1936, 1937.
I have no idea what her name is.
She lived in Rotterdam, and she came to visit.
I believe this picture was taken after my parents were married, and she went back to Holland and we never saw her again because she was deported in 1942, I think.
